data.title: Richtech Robotics Is A Dilution Trap (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The first time I broke down Richtech Robotics Inc. (RR) was back in October, as I was looking for a robotic stock to invest in myself. After reviewing the company's numbers, I gave it a Hold rating and noted that a $1.1 billion market cap, combined with low single-digit revenue, did not make much sense. I remind you, the stock of the company has traded at about 280x EV/sales, 10 to 20 times higher than most of the robotics competitors. Since then, the valuation has cooled down, and now the market cap is below $800 million, with 166 EV/sales on a trailing twelve-month basis.

Not that it’s cheap, but it’s definitely better, and together with the fact that the company just released its 10-K for 2025, I think it’s the perfect moment to go through that filing. And, oh boy, did I find it interesting.

I don’t remember the last time I read the entire 10-K, and I was reading it faster and faster as my eyes widened from the disbelief. Richtech Robotics managed to surprise me once again, but not to impose my personal opinion on you, I’ll walk you through the report so you can see the numbers for yourself. Before getting to the section where I explain my standpoint on the findings, you will make up your own mind. Let’s call it an interactive article, because I am genuinely curious whether my reaction is proportionate.

10-K 2025

Going through Richtech Robotics' fiscal year 2025, we see that the company ended the year with net revenue of $5.05 million. It was up by about 19% from the $4.24 million one year ago. If you break that down to see where the money is coming from, product sales brought in $2.31 million, while leasing and services added $1.43 million, and their Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS) model contributed just shy of $700,000.

Jumping from here to the income statement, we see that after accounting for the cost of revenue at $1.76 million, the company was left with a gross profit of roughly $3.29 million, which was actually a better result percentage-wise than the last year. According to my calculation, it means that the gross margin of the company expanded by about 100 basis points. Research and Development spending increased but only slightly to $2.43 million from $2.02 million, while Sales and Marketing expenses actually decreased to $1.26 million from $1.32 million. General and Administrative costs surged to $17.54 million, a massive increase from the $6.46 million spent in 2024. When you subtract total operating expenses from the gross profit, you get a loss from operations of $17.94 million and a net loss for the year of $15.75 million.

Despite the losses, the company’s bank account looks drastically different than it did a year ago. They ended 2025 with $193.6 million in cash, while a year ago its cash position had been at about $14.6 million.

We already know that this cash didn’t come from robot sales from the income statement, so we have to look for another table to see where it came from.

It came from financing activities, which poured in a net $236.1 million, mostly through selling Class B shares via "At-The-Market" offerings. Speaking of shares, the dilution is visible in the count, with about 40 million Class A shares and over 175 million Class B shares outstanding as of January 2026.

Applicable percentage ownership is based on an aggregate of 215,095,973 shares of our common stock, consisting of (i) 39,934,846 shares of our Class A common stock and (ii) 175,161,127 shares of our Class B common stock outstanding as of January 20, 2026. We have determined beneficial ownership in accordance with the rules of the SEC. - 10-K 2025

Here is a quote from my previous article for the comparison:

As of August 2025, there were about 39.9 million Class A shares and 113 million Class B shares, representing 153 million total votes.

On the human capital, the company is running with a team of 55 full-time employees in the United States, mostly based in Nevada. The breakdown of that team shows the focus is heavily weighted toward engineering, with 27 people in R&D, compared to 12 in Sales and Marketing, 8 in Administration, and just a handful in Operations and Tech Support. They also supplement this with 20 overseas R&D staff, primarily out of China.

This was pretty much the main part of the 10-K that was worth noticing. And now, let me tell you how I view those results.

Company Overview

The first question I had when I saw the revenue mix was how it could be that leasing and rental were down while RaaS is up? Isn’t it the same as leasing and rental? The answer is nuanced, and while there is nothing particularly bad, I really don’t like it when the company starts “playing with the numbers” on their books, even if legally they don’t cross any borders.

So what happened there is that they decided to label the revenue streams differently this year versus last year. According to the filing, "Leasing/Service/Rental" and "RaaS" are treated as two different sources. The "Leasing" category will be a category for all the short-term contracts, including maintenance and installation fees, whereas under the "RaaS," you will find their long-term operating agreements. And it would not be a big deal if it did not change the way this company recognizes the revenue, because from here, we’ve got the expanded margins.

Gross profit increased by $569 thousand, or approximately 20.9%, from $2,720 thousand in 2024 to $3,289 thousand in 2025. The resulting expansion of our gross margin is a direct reflection of the full-year impact of the strategic shift to a RaaS model. By capitalizing the cost of leased assets rather than recognizing them as immediate cost of goods sold, our gross margin profile has significantly improved, leading to a higher gross profit despite the ongoing business model transition.

Translating this to simple language, they used an accounting trick to lift their gross margins. When they sell a robot, they have to subtract the full cost of building it immediately, which hurts their profit for that period. But under the new "Robots-as-a-Service" rental model, they get to include manufacturing cost on the balance sheet as an asset and only subtract a small part of it each month over time as depreciation and amortization. It makes their gross profit look significantly higher today, even though in practice, they spend the same amount of money, but they are not supposed to recognize it straight away. While this is not forbidden, a move like this can easily go unnoticed by the market, confusing real growth with shifts in accounting strategy.

In my previous article, my main critique point of this company was the fact that they spend too little on R&D and too much on other expenses that do not contribute to the real innovation and growth of this company in the future:

Spending nearly $14 million in G&A with only $3.6 million in revenue is not sustainable, especially when R&D remains barely above $1 million. If Richtech wants to compete in robotics long-term, it needs to stop throwing money on short-term marketing and start heavily working on innovation, because otherwise, this will be the company that will run out of fuel in a few years and go bankrupt. Without new technologies, no one would even want to acquire them.

I thought it was already bad then, but I couldn’t imagine it would become so much worse. The company slightly increased the R&D spending year-over-year, meaning almost no spending in the last quarter of 2025, because the R&D in September for the year 2025 had already been at $2.4 million. How this company creates something without investing in it is beyond me. Marketing decreased, but only a little, which is actually good, taking into account how huge the expense already was before.

But what happened with the General and Administrative expenses? This is the expense that I, as an investor, don’t want to see ballooning at all. This is the type of expense that doesn’t help the company with its operating activities and doesn’t make the product better. In this category, you usually have: Rent, Utilities, Insurance, Executives' wages and benefits, Office fixtures and equipment (not related to manufacturing), Legal counsel and accounting staff salaries, Office supplies.

The company states that the increase is primarily due to legal, audit, and consulting costs as a result of being a public company and the requirements for all the SEC filings, etc. But if you actually look at the statement, you would see that they are not required to provide checks to where the majority of the money actually went. Not to accuse anyone of anything, I want to underline that throughout the whole year of 2024, the company has already been public and had the same requirements for legal paperwork.

They also say they had additional spending on issuance, which could happen, although the ballooning of the expense is too big to justify in my view. So the most probable cause, in my opinion, and it is also stated in the 10-K, is the purchase of the new corporate headquarters and the costs for moving.

Now, I have a serious concern that this has nothing to do with the growth of the company and the real need for that improvement. To address this concern, I found the filing of the company from a year ago, for fiscal 2024. It turns out they not only have fewer employees today than they did back then, but I was once again disappointed to see that their R&D department shrank by 25% in a year. How does that make sense for the innovative company that positions itself to make any kind of breakthrough? I also read that the management says they have difficulties in hiring talent, especially in R&D, but I have an idea why they encounter such a problem - it looks like they have a huge employee turnover ratio, and based on the feedback online on the trusted websites, I conclude that this is not the best place to work in.

If I were a shareholder, I would be seriously disappointed by the enormous dilution of my value for covering something as insignificant as a new headquarters.

I also ask myself what this enormous cash position is for. New furniture is great, but it looks like this company is planning on a larger expense than that. On one hand, in the best interest of shareholders, I would hope that the plan is to make a strategic acquisition, since the company shrinks its R&D department and doesn’t seem to be innovating by itself. On the other hand, their return from investing is negative, so where are any guarantees that the money will be spent correctly and that the execution of this acquisition will be solid?

Valuation

About the valuation, the stock is majorly overvalued, and even though the revenue is growing, a 20% growth rate is nowhere near justifying this premium. Looking at the expenses, in my view this company is apparently not prioritizing shareholder value or innovation. I am not convinced about all the products and developments this company has. It states that the product is deployed to many customers throughout the U.S., yet I was unable to find a single piece of feedback on how it works and how much value it brings to the businesses, which I find odd.

Conclusion

Taking into account everything, I found this RR breakdown really difficult, as the numbers show a story that is hard to tell without reading too much into it. I am not an insider, and it’s possible I interpret the numbers in the wrong way, but being honest, this doesn’t look good to me as an investor. Repeating the beginning of this piece, I don’t remember when I was reading the report with such enthusiasm, not believing what I saw. The company managed to beat the expectations, and it looks like the stock is climbing higher on the news (it's up by almost 2% on the premarket as I am writing this), but I see something entirely different. I know betting against the hyped stocks in hot industries is burning your money, but Richtech Robotics, as it is today, doesn’t deserve any premium, let alone 5000% on the sector median.

data.title: Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
This note will highlight the results of the Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund for Q4 2025. It is published amidst the ongoing developments associated with the Russian war with Ukraine, ongoing inflation concerns in the U.S., and significant government policy changes in the U.S. and elsewhere that are altering global trade and financial flows. Most equity indices worldwide delivered positive returns in the December quarter and for calendar 2025. Returns from bonds in the U.S. were positive.

Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund (TIBIX) paid ordinary dividends of $0.42 per I share during Q4’2025, flat versus Q4’24. For calendar 2025 your fund paid ordinary dividends of $1.27 per I share, +2.6% vs the prior year. The dividends per share were lower for A and C shares, to account for varying class specific expenses. Your fund also paid a long-term capital gains dividend of 66.2¢/share in November 2025. Including the capital gains dividends, trailing 4 quarter dividends of $1.93 per I share were +29.5% vs the combined dividend of $1.49 per share for the 4 quarters ending December 31, 2024.

Approximately 90% of the dividends paid by your fund in 2024 were characterized as “Qualifying Dividends” for federal income tax purposes, leading to a lower tax rate on these payments. When 2025 calculations are finalized, we expect a slightly smaller percentage of your fund’s 2025 distributions to also be qualifying dividends. More than 75% of your Investment Income Builder’s equity holdings increased dividends in local currencies in 2025. A weaker $US was a tailwind to your fund’s dividend growth in calendar 2025, though reduced special dividend payments were a head- wind. Other factors may impact the distributable cash total.

The net asset value of Investment Income Builder’s I shares increased by +$7.41 per share ($25.78 to $33.19) over the trailing 12 months and increased by +$1.08 per share ($32.11 to $33.19) during the December quarter. Your fund’s 12/31/25 net asset value was reduced by the aforementioned 66¢/share capital gains dividends paid in Q4’25.

We have maintained your portfolio’s exposure to dividend-paying firms that we believe have resilient businesses with strong capital structures.

Investment Income Builder’s I share return of 6.75% for Q4 2025 exceeded the blended benchmark return of 2.61% for the December quarter (the blended benchmark is 75% MSCI World Index and 25% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index). For the 12-month period ending December 31, 2025, Investment Income Builder’s I share return of 36.93% exceeded the blended bench- mark return of 17.60%. Performance comparisons of Investment Income Builder to its blended benchmark over various periods are shown on page 1.

The quarter ending December 31, 2025, was the 92nd full calendar quarter since the inception of Thornburg Investment Income Builder in December 2002. In 68 of these quarters the fund delivered a positive total return. The fund has delivered positive total returns in 18 of its 23 calendar years of existence.

Importantly, for an investor reinvesting dividends Thornburg Investment Income Builder has delivered an average annual total return of 10.4% since its inception 23 years ago, split between quarterly income distributions and share price appreciation.

On a $100,000 initial investment a hypothetical investor owning Thornburg Investment Income Builder A shares and taking all ordinary dividends in cash (no reinvestment of quarterly dividends) would have received total cash dividends of $196,170 over these 92 quarters, an average of $8,529 per year over 23 years. The calendar 2025 cash dividend was $10,856. In addition, the current value of the original $100,000 investment stood at $303,260 as of December 31, 2025.

Together, these 25 firms comprised approximately 68% of the fund’s total assets as of December 31, 2025. Cash and approximately 250 interest-bearing debt and hybrid securities comprised around 16% of fund assets, and 31 other common equities comprised a total of approximately 16% of fund assets. Twenty equity investments contributed positive returns of at least 0.10% to overall Income Builder Fund portfolio performance during Q4’2025. The most positive equity contributors to quarterly performance included telecommunications network operators Orange (ORANY), Vodafone (VOD), Singapore Telecommunications (SGAPY) and Zegona Communications (ZEGLF); information technology firms Broadcom (AVGO), Samsung (SSNLF), Cisco Systems (CSCO), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM); financials Citigroup (C), BNP Paribas (BNPQF), NN Group (NNGPF), and Bank of Ireland (BKRIF); pharmaceutical firms Merck & Co (MRK), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Roche Holding (RHHBY); European utilities ENEL (ENLAY) and Endesa (ELEZF); miner Glencore (GLCNF); and DHL Group and TotalEnergies (TTE).

Three of your fund’s equity investments made negative contributions to portfolio performance of more than -0.10% during Q4’2025. These negative contributors to quarterly performance included Home Depot (HD), BAE Systems (BAESF), and AT&T (T).

Investment Income Builder’s bond holdings delivered positive returns during Q4’2025. Ten-year U.S. Treasury bond yields inched higher from 4.15% to 4.17% in Q4’2025 and dropped -0.4% from 4.57% during calendar 2025. Corporate and asset-backed bond yield spreads to U.S. Treasury bonds tightened in 2025. The market yield of the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index decreased from 6.69% to 6.53% during the December quarter (yield to lower of call price or maturity), after earlier declining from 7.49% as of December 31, 2024. On December 31, 2025, the $74.6 trillion Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index showed an average maturity of 8.2 years and a yield of 3.52%, the latter increasing by +0.04% over the December quarter. Developed world bond yields outside the U.S. are mostly below those available in the U.S. market.

Readers of this commentary who are long-time shareholders of Thornburg Investment Income Builder will recall that the interest-bearing debt portion of the fund’s portfolio has varied over time, ranging from less than 9% in 2015 to 45% on June 30, 2009. We tend to allocate more portfolio assets to interest-bearing debt when debt yields are more attractive. Approximately 16% of portfolio assets were invested in interest-bearing debt and near cash assets on December 31, 2025. The remaining 84% of portfolio assets were invested in equities as previously described.

The outlook for financial asset returns is uncertain. Earnings and other valuation multiples for most segments of the U.S. equity market are above historic averages though sector multiples vary across the globe. Real wages after inflation for a large segment of U.S. households are not increasing. The absolute number of unemployed workers in the U.S. has been rising, making additional Federal Funds rate cuts probable despite inflation running above Federal Reserve targets. Recent progress to reach the Fed’s 2% inflation target has been difficult to achieve, complicated by uncertainty around tariffs and curtailments in the number of foreign-born workers in the U.S. labor force. A large supply of new government debt being issued plus ongoing corporate borrowing present challenges to further reductions in longer maturity bond yields unless the U.S. economy weakens considerably in the coming quarters.

Incoming economic data from around the world indicate an uncertain global economy in the face of evolving U.S. trade policies, so far resisting falling into a recession. Global trade flows are being altered by new U.S. trade policies. Winners and losers among multinational producers of tradeable goods and developers of artificial intelligence capabilities will become obvious in time, but the current outlook for many global businesses is uncertain. Prices of traded financial assets will be volatile with day-to-day news changing perceptions of near-term economic performance, interest rate policies, and political decisions. We are optimistic about the future return potential of Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s assets. Why?

Virtually all the businesses in your portfolio retain their market positions providing important products and services that generate cash flows to pay attractive dividends, today and in the future. We believe they are valued very attractively in relation to their own histories and relative to other assets.

The weighted harmonic average 2025 consensus expected price/earnings ratio for Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s equity portfolio, tabulated using FactSet data, was approximately 14.2x as of December 31, 2025, well below the 21.6x price/earnings ratio of the MSCI All Country World Index using 2025 expected EPS. Income Builder’s 4.2% weighted average equity portfolio dividend yield significantly exceeds the 1.7% dividend yield of the MSCI Index. We believe your Investment Income Builder portfolio incorporates significant intrinsic value. The bond portion of your fund’s portfolio has a duration of approximately 3.7 years and a yield to maturity/call of approximately 6.6%.

Thank you for being a shareholder of Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund. Remember that you can review additional information about your portfolio by going to our website, www.thornburg.com.

Important Information

Unless otherwise noted, the source of all data, charts, tables and graphs is Thornburg Investment Management, Inc., as of 31 Dec 2025.

Investments carry risks, including possible loss of principal. Additional risks may be associated with investments outside the United States, especially in emerging markets, including currency fluctuations, illiquidity, volatility, and political and economic risks. Investments in small- and mid-capitalization companies may increase the risk of greater price fluctuations. Portfolios investing in bonds have the same interest rate, inflation, and credit risks that are associated with the underlying bonds. The value of bonds will fluctuate relative to changes in interest rates, decreasing when interest rates rise. Investments in the Fund are not FDIC insured, nor are they bank deposits or guaranteed by a bank or any other entity.

data.title: I See An Opening, 2 BDCs To Buy
data.description:
BDCs (BIZD) have started this year strong. The sector has so far outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), which embody the promising AI factor.

I think that it would be fair to say that for many this kind of BDC performance has come as a surprise given the headwinds from lower interest rates, which will drag down earnings in at least Q4, 2025 and Q1, 2026 earnings reports.

While I completely agree that the overall macro/structural environment is not the best one for BDCs, seeing the BDC outperformance is what I internally expected. The key driver is the aggressive discount factor that has been assigned to most BDCs - currently, the sector median P/NAV is 0.83x (i.e., we can buy BDC net assets at $0.83 cents on a dollar). Whenever there is this kind of discount in place, which is combined with positive incoming data, the reaction function tends to be quite favorable.

In this case, what has worked in favor of BDCs is the following:

  • Lower probabilities for the next interest cut happening soon.
  • Rotation into small caps and value.
  • Improving labor market.

Now, I hope that this won't sound like a brag, but it is good to see that my Top 3 BDC picks (my closest followers are most likely aware of that) - Fidus Investment (FDUS), Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN), and Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) - have all outperformed the BDC index:

It is good to see that the market is appreciating high-quality BDCs (in terms of capital structure, earnings profile, and portfolio composition).

In fact, if we look at the entire publicly traded BDC space, we will see that there are only four BDCs that have not produced positive returns so far.

Among these four BDCs, we have FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) and Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF), which, in my view, present attractive investment opportunities:

I view this divergence as an opening for investors to consider establishing positions in these names with potentially abnormal returns.

Let me explain.

Wonderful Opening to Invest in FSK and OTF

FSK and OTF are both externally managed BDCs with a focus on middle market lending. Just as most of their peers that have the portfolios tilted towards first and second-lien lending (with limited equity co-investment exposures), the P/NAV multiples are in the discount territory.

The P/NAV for FSK and OTF stand at 0.84x and 0.67x, respectively. These are massive discounts, especially for FSK, which is exactly what provides an opportunity for making smart, balanced tactical investments.

The drivers for the elevated risk premiums that have been assigned for these two BDCs are different for each of them.

For FSK, it is primarily about non-accruals, which historically have inflicted serious damage to FSK's NAV. So, the concern that the market has is theoretically logical, as weak portfolio quality is arguably the biggest risk in the BDC business. However, there is an important caveat that we have to take into account. I think that the market is missing this (I will explain that a bit later in the article).

For OTF, the risk could be related to high exposure to PIK (13.8% of total income) and a meaningful chunk of income stemming from recurring revenue loans, which are made to EBITDA-negative businesses (i.e., risky businesses). Plus, and I think that this plays a bigger role - OTF has several IPO lock-up dates ahead, which upon expiry will bring new shares (supply) to the market.

In a nutshell, these could be the bear arguments for the market to price FSK and OTF in a way that is not that shareholder-friendly.

Now to my points, which justify my bullish stance on these two BDCs.

Speaking of FSK, the main source of the historical non-accrual problems came from the externally acquired portfolio, which has been largely already churned out and replaced with KKR Credit originated/underwritten investments. The KKR Credit part of the portfolio has performed in line with the sector average and currently has even better non-accrual statistics than the rest (average) of the sector. Here the following excerpt from the Q3 earnings call is quite relevant:

We also believe it is helpful to provide the market with information based upon FSK's assets originated by KKR Credit. Nonaccruals relating to the 90% of the portfolio, which has been originated by KKR Credit and the FS/KKR Advisor, were 3.4% on a cost basis and 1.8% on a fair value basis as of the end of the third quarter. This compares to 3.8% on a cost basis and 2% on a fair value basis as of the end of the second quarter.

On top of that, FSK portfolio companies have been delivering growing EBITDA results, which, in combination with a lower interest rate environment (cheaper debt service for companies), should further de-risk the non-accrual part.

Apart from the non-accrual risk part, which I think is certainly a much smaller issue than before, I do not see any reason why FSK should be priced so cheaply. The debt-to-equity stands at 1.16x, which is below the sector average. The weighted average yield (and spread) on debt investments is not far away from what FSK can source from the market now (i.e., spread compression headwinds are immaterial). The portfolio median interest coverage levels have been ticking up for a couple of quarters in a row, and the most recent quarter (Q3, 2025) resulted in a positive net realized and unrealized gain, putting a stop to the previous declines that were mostly coming from the externally acquired portfolio assets.

We have to ask ourselves a question whether with this kind of backdrop in mind, FSK deserves being discounted 33% below its book value. It could be justified if the non-accruals continue to emerge, but looking at the current data points, I simply do not see a case for that. In my view, if FSK manages to convince the market that the non-accrual story is over by delivering strong Q4, 2025 and Q1, 2026 reports, we should be able to capture sizeable profits from the valuation multiple recovery while collecting a dividend of 13% - 14%, which we can imply from the 10% NAV yield guidance that the Management gave in the recent earnings call.

Speaking of OTF, the story is a bit different.

OTF has not faced any headwinds from unfavorable non-accruals. In fact, based on the current non-accruals statistics, we could argue that OTF is one of the highest quality BDCs out there - i.e., as of Q3, 2025 OTF had 0.2% in non-accruals, while the sector average was 3.6%. For OTF the 0.2% figure has remained stable for the last 4 quarters, which is not a bad sign.

As stated above, the pressure on the multiple comes from primarily high PIK and new supply of shares - both of which are, in my view, completely acceptable in the context of current P/NAV and long-term investing approach.

When it comes to PIK, then there are three important facts to appreciate:

  • About 97% of the PIK loan investments have been structured as such from the get-go (i.e., strategy and not non-accrual driven). Also, based on the management's commentary, OTF has never experienced a non-accrual from a structured PIK loan investment.
  • The weighted average LTV of the recurring revenue loan investments is only 19%, and they are all supported by private equity investors.
  • In Q3, 2025, we could notice how OTF increased its NAV due to positive valuation marks.

I would even say that OTF deserves some form of a premium given its very conservative debt-to-equity ratio, just 0.57x. From this level, OTF can significantly expand its loan book without entering into a financially risky zone. And as we know, the larger the loan book, the higher the total spread value that can be captured and fed into the NII per share generation.

In other words, I do not see a fundamental-driven argument that could support OTF's discount and the recent (YTD) underperformance.

So, the main driver or argument that I could buy is the following:

OTF has in front of it four dates in which a material chunk of shares will be released to the market. The total number of shares accounts for ~40% of the total outstanding float.

From the pure supply and demand perspective, the share price is not in too positive a spot. Yet, as explained above, fundamentally the BDC is very sound and arguably strong enough to carry a relative premium.

For short-term traders, it might be an interesting opportunity to bet on dips that might emerge right around these lock-up dates.

For long-term investors, however, the current discount provides a clear opportunity to scoop up the BDC business below its intrinsic value (remember, these shares that will be released to the public market are already active - i.e., financial metrics on a per share won't be diluted).

data.title: Eli Lilly: Buy Ahead Of Its Earnings Day (Preview)
data.description:
I'm Still Bullish on LLY Despite Its Seeming Overvaluation

I have noticed that these days, Eli Lilly (LLY) does not get the same level of attention it used to before everyone started caring about AI and tech stocks in general. As a result, LLY has started to look like a silent winner, although its primary obesity end market has been going through many changes that seem to have reshaped the landscape for Eli. That is why the stock corrected throughout most of 2025 - the market was repricing its prospects. However, LLY has managed to beat the S&P 500 index (SPY) anyway, proving that the bullish thesis is still alive and running.

I have written only two articles on LLY stock, the latest published in August 2025 with a "Buy" rating, which could have delivered 37-38% in total returns for buyers. While it looks a bit too expensive for the level of competition in its main selling market to date, I think that Eli Lilly remains one of the best picks for what its business can show over the next few years. Ahead of the upcoming earnings report, which should come out in about 2 weeks from now, I see some unique catalysts that should drive the stock higher, supporting the current rally despite the seeming overvaluation. I remain bullish on LLY.

Why Do I Think So?

Eli Lilly reported its Q3 2025 results in late October, showing $17.6 billion in revenues (+54% YoY, a beat of 9.53%) and $7.02 in adjusted EPS (+495% YoY, a beat of 19.15%). The stock price closed slightly higher after the release, up about 3%, but then a strong recovery took place and drove LLY up by 28% as of today. Since the firm has managed to achieve relatively strong beats in Q3, and the management's commentary was largely positive (I'll discuss them below), Wall Street analysts, almost all of whom cover the stock, decided to raise the estimates for Q4:

The key point is that LLY did not see positive revisions just for its upcoming Q4 - the strength of revisions was broad-based, and the most bullish updates came in LLY's long-term estimates (FY2028-2031). We can clearly see that from Seeking Alpha's data and the underlying revision trends:

In my opinion, we're now looking at a major disconnection with long-term consensus earnings estimates consistently migrating upward, and the stock price still failing to reflect this expanded earnings power. Yes, LLY gained almost 30% after the Q3 print, but I don't see it as a sufficient reward from market participants since the multiple compression seems to have gone too far.

Right now, LLY trades at less than 32x FY2026 EPS, and the implied P/E multiple after all the revisions done has effectively dropped below 26x for FY2027:

It's getting pretty close to the minimum P/E multiple of the past decade (on the forward basis) if we look at YCharts' data:

Curious readers might ask, "Probably LLY's growth profile has deteriorated, and that explains the multiple compressions?"

When we take into account the TTM financials and look back at the past results, we see that LLY's revenues have been growing at a 10-year CAGR of 11.52%, while its non-GAAP EPS has had a CAGR of ~23.8% for the same period. The current consensus estimates that LLY's revenues and EPS figures will reach $120.06 billion and ~$59.06 by the end of 2030, respectively. Based on the FY2025 consensus, the 5-year forward CAGRs are going to be at about 13.51% and 20.15%. So, the difference between what LLY has already gone through and what's coming next isn't that massive, in my view. As a result, the stock has become too cheap on a forward basis - I see no overvaluation at all because the level of growth should basically stay close to the past norms.

One can ask, "How will LLY be able to keep these massive business expansion rates for so long?"

And that would be a great question. I see some concrete catalysts, which should help the company do it.

The next phase of growth seems to be defined by orforglipron and retatrutide. While Zepbound and Mounjaro (tirzepatide) have managed to generate about $24.8 billion combined in the first 9 months of 2025 alone, I think we should focus on the former two.

Orforglipron in particular is a non-peptide oral GLP-1 receptor agonist, which is its key distinction from Novo Nordisk's (NVO) oral Wegovy, because the latter requires an empty stomach as it's peptide-based.

What drove down LLY's stock price in the recent trading session was the delay in the FDA decision on orforglipron to April 10, 2026. But I view it as just noise because this delay overlooks the fundamental manufacturing advantage of orforglipron over its peers on the market - the small-molecule nature it has allows LLY to manufacture it at lower pricing and in higher quantities at the same time. This feature will likely help LLY capture the lion's share of the obesity market in the future because it can stay more flexible on pricing.

But if orforglipron is the tool for mass-market expansion, the other drug that I mentioned initially - retatrutide - is the key instrument for clinical dominance. It's a triple hormone receptor agonist (GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon), targeting 3 distinct pathways to manage obesity and type 2 diabetes. In late 2025, we could have seen that retatrutide's Phase 3 TRIUMPH-4 trial results were absolutely spectacular with an average weight loss of up to 28.7% among patients after 68 weeks.

According to Gran View Research's study, the global GLP-1 agonist weight loss drugs market should expand with a CAGR of 18.54% over the next few years, ultimately reaching $48.84 billion in size by 2030. Mordor Intelligence analysts see the size of the incretin-based drugs market at $51.19 billion by 2031. If Mordor's data is to be believed, the current trends in LLY's market share should bring in ~$31 billion in FY2030 sales alone (assuming a further rise to 60% market share by that time).

In addition to just orforglipron and retatrutide, Eli has a tremendous pipeline under its belt, with most of its prospective drugs/products being at Phase 3 as of October 2025:

Another catalyst that should drive volumes for LLY is the firm's agreement with the U.S. government, which was signed in late 2025. According to the terms, both sides reached a "volume-for-price" trade-off, which I think should dramatically expand the addressable market for LLY as it will start targeting another 40 million people. We know that LLY's obesity treatments will be covered under Medicare for the first time for patients with specific comorbidities, so the serviceable market has been effectively boosted, in my understanding.

From all that, I think LLY has all the chances for seeing more positive revisions to its 2027-2030 EPS estimates.

If we see only a 5% premium in FY2027 EPS growth rate and assume a 30x earnings multiple on that EPS, we'll get a price target of $1,280.5/share (12-18 months horizon). That's about 23% above the current stock price, and it sits between the high and midpoint of Wall Street's consensus price target, according to Seeking Alpha:

I keep my "Buy" on LLY in place for now.

The Upcoming Q4 Earnings Review

In the short term, the main catalyst is the upcoming earnings release, which should come out in about two weeks. Based on the FY2025 updated guidance, we can see that Q4 sales and EPS headline figures are projected at $17.36 billion and $6.68 at their midpoints, respectively.

Based on Seeking Alpha's data, the consensus targets are above the high end of management's guidance (both on revenues and EPS):

But the premiums the current consensus assumes are quite low; they're at 0.85% for EPS and at 1.42% for the revenue. The double-beating in Q3 looks too excessive for these premiums to threaten this time, in my opinion. We have to remember that the late 2025 deal with the U.S. government will expand the market for LLY in 2026, and hence the odds for more positive commentary on the upcoming call have risen significantly. I expect another beating in Q4 and positive commentary from the management as well, with possibly some key updates on the pipeline's developments/plans.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

I think we should be skeptical, even while being bullish, on LLY and its prospects and maintain a cold eye. First, the level of competition in the space is rising, so even though LLY keeps the leading position for now, it might lose it over time - especially if NVO's alternative drugs prove to be more efficient. Yes, LLY's manufacturing ability has an advantage over NVO in the U.S. market, but it might not matter as much as efficacy and pricing. Novo has already got its first-mover advantage by launching its oral Wegovy pill in early January 2026 (again, the FDA delayed its decision on LLY's pills by April 2026). If the FDA commission decides to extend that delay for another few months, Eli might lose its market share quite quickly or under-earn a significant portion.

Another risk lies in LLY's stock price, which might be quite expensive indeed for the healthcare space. Even after the P/E's contraction in the past few years, LLY can't be called cheap on an absolute P/E basis, even when we look at its FY2026 EPS figures. The growth fully justifies the multiples, in my opinion, but I may be wrong here - if LLY's P/E drops to 20x by the end of FY2027, and the current EPS consensus is right, the implied price target would drop to $813/share.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I think that LLY is going through a massive bullish cycle, and its volume expansion opportunities have only gotten stronger in the medium term. Although I don't doubt that the FDA's delay in decision on orforglipron shouldn't be blindly ignored, I actually like that the stock's price has dropped because the forward earnings estimates have only improved. I see 23% upside in the next 12-18 months, so I remain bullish on LLY a couple of weeks ahead of its Q4 earnings release.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: REITs Were Left Behind In 2025 - Why Realty Income's Setup Now Looks Better
data.description:
The performance of REITs in 2025, gauged against other sectors in the S&P 500, was simply an Achilles' heel with flat performance year-over-year.

Seeking Alpha is full of articles focused on REITs - as they provide a very attractive yield to income-seeking investors, but the reality is, in the last 1-2 years, you would earn a better yield on cash in US treasuries, without risk.

The higher-for-longer narratives have played out, and real estate, being among the most rate-sensitive sectors, took the hit. But, it's fair to say that also after this time, the valuations remain compressed and opportunities are opening up, especially if we gauge it against premium valuations in other sectors.

Fed's path to lower rates won't be linear in 2026, but it's likely to continue: 1-2 rate cuts, each probably a reduction by 0.25 basis points. Fed now also restarted buying back treasuries, which should support lower short-term and long-term rates, which should boost REITs valuations. At the same time as treasury yields fall, investors seeking yield could rotate back to REITs/equities with above-average dividend distribution.

The combination of improved refinancing landscape, lower rates, improved cap rates, and better cash flows for real estate firms in 2026 could all lead to a strong year for the industry, and a firm like Realty Income Corporation (O), with its valuation still depressed, could be one of the main beneficiaries.

Business Update

If we look back at Q3 performance, Realty Income reported $1.39B in revenue or $1.07 AFFO.

The stable performance over the last quarters, despite the inferior rate cycle, continues to support the narrative that so many income-oriented investors keep the asset in their portfolios.

That's despite the fact that if you held O's shares from the start of 2016 until today, you would earn just 5.1% total ROR (incl. dividends) annually.

If you gauge it against the S&P 500's 12-14% annual return over this time period, that's simply leaving money on the table.

Simply, Realty Income is trading like a bond, and it should be treated in a portfolio like one. The REIT received quite a lot of negativity on Seeking Alpha for the perceived lack of performance, and while part of the critique is warranted, we shouldn't overlook the real objective of the asset—a stability.

Despite the inferior cycle and what we could call a deterioration of tenant quality for O, with firms like Dollar General Corporation (DG) and Walgreens facing an existential crisis due to consumers on the lower spectrum of income reducing spending while operating outdated business models, O's occupancy remained very high—at 98.7%.

The resiliency of its portfolio is confirmed quarter after quarter, as its average occupancy since 2000 remained at 98.3%, well-above the S&P 500's average of 94.4%. This is a result of careful underwriting during acquisitions, diversified clientele, and long-term leases. But what I see as more important is the behavior during challenging times. E.g., in 2007-2013, its occupancy didn't dip below 96%—still 1.40 points above the S&P 500 average.

My argument is further supported by O's AFFO resiliency. In the last 18 years, its bottom line contracted only in 2008-2009 during the GFC by 2% in each of those years. Outside of this troubled period, O's AFFO grew on average 4.72%, which supports the argument of resiliency at its finest.

Realty Income remains among the largest REITs with a market cap of $56.5B, managing 15k+ properties with 1,647 clients across 92 industries.

Looking for this type of diversification elsewhere is rather a futile effort. This supports the company's financial resiliency, occupancy, and ultimately the high distribution—but also introduces another risk of lackluster growth due to its size.

The Growth & Valuation

Let's talk about the growth.

If we look back at Q3 numbers, we see the firm invested $1.4B with an average cash yield of 7.7%. Over 70% of this capital was deployed in European markets, and in fact, the yield there was 8.0%. This shows that the firm is very active in the market, where it's still relatively underrepresented compared to the US. Currently, roughly 17% of O's portfolio (in terms of rent collected) is in the UK & Europe.

At 7.7-8.0%, we can assume these new net leases will be AFFO accretive for the firm—even if financing rates aren't currently the most favorable.

We'll shortly see the Q4 results (and full-year 2025), but the company's management is expecting full-year AFFO per share of $4.25-4.27. In 2024, O reported $4.19 AFFO/share, hence we are likely to see a growth of 1.5-2.0% this year. That's slower than 4.8% growth we've seen from 2023 to 2024.

It isn't ideal. Fact Set forecast is similar to the full-year AFFO of $4.27 as O's management. We simply see that the restrictive Fed policy continues to weigh on the results as the spread between the cost of debt and return from property rents is limited.

If we held O's valuation constant: 1.5% AFFO growth + 5.3% dividend yield = total ROR of 6.8%, which I certainly see as lower than what the broader market could deliver. This is a stalwart-type of result, a bond-like one as I said earlier.

The good thing is that all of this is priced in and has been for some time. That's why I always say it's important to buy Realty Income at a discount. Valuation-wise, you can expect lackluster results.

O's issue is the size. At a $56.5B market valuation, it's becoming increasingly more difficult to find opportunities that will move the needle. It's a similar story to Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A)(BRK.B). That's why O is exploring new opportunities in new areas, such as data centers, gambling, and pushing into the UK & Europe. This increases the risk of poor judgment or could lead to deterioration in tenant quality long-term, but we aren't seeing this development play out for now.

As an example, Realty Income formed a joint venture with Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR) investing in Virginia into data centers. O's stake is 80%, from the ≈$200M investment.

FactSet is forecasting the following AFFO growth in the next years:

  • 2025: AFFO of $4.27E, YoY growth of 2%
  • 2026: AFFO of $4.41E, YoY growth of 3%
  • 2027: AFFO of $4.53E, YoY growth of 3%
  • 2028: AFFO of $4.65E, YoY growth of 3%

You see, slow and steady. The expectation hasn't changed much throughout 2025.

A few of its peers are expected to grow faster. For instance, Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) is expected to grow its AFFO ≈5% annually, and W. P. Carey Inc. (WPC) 3-5% annually.

The positive catalyst could be reduced interest rates in 2026, which would unlock faster growth and a higher valuation. This isn't guaranteed—far from it. But all the negative headlines are priced in, and any positive news could unlock the upside.

Since my last coverage from back in August, O's shares are up 7.5%, which impacted the valuation slightly.

Currently, the stock is priced at 14.37x its P/AFFO. Its 15-year average is 18x, giving you a sense of the discount.

However, I don't see 18x AFFO as being realistic going forward. O's growth forward will be ≈3% annually, lower than the 15Y average of 4%, and this is the main culprit why I think O's future fair valuation should be ≈15x AFFO.

Again, the growth may be revised higher if rates fall and O can once again capture a wider spread between debt/return, but this is wishful thinking for now.

The potential upside here is ≈9-10% annually (incl. dividends), which I view as favorable for income-seeking investors.

Realty Income's Dividend

We can't skip a discussion about O's dividend.

The firm is largely considered the golden standard of dividend distribution, given its high yield and monthly payouts.

O's dividend is currently 5.28%.

From the table below, you can see it isn't the highest in the last 24 months, but we are in the upper range of its historical distribution.

Perhaps you are asking about the dividend growth, and rightfully so, especially in the face of higher inflation, when offsetting the loss of purchasing power is becoming even more important.

Historically, O's dividend grew 3-4% annually, which was enough to offset the average inflation between 1995 and 2025, but you wouldn't call it a dividend-grower stock per se.

The firm increased dividends 133 times in a row, but even as we'll see increases in the future, if AFFO growth will be ≈3% annually, that will be the maximum we can see dividend-wise as well.

The current dividend stands at $0.27/month - last time increased in December from $0.2695.

Investor's Takeaway

Realty Income remains a core holding for many income-seeking investors.

Despite the inferior interest rate cycle and headwinds with a few of its tenants, the occupancy remains very high, with results showing minor year-over-year growth.

Realty Income is stepping into new industries, which could introduce execution risk and long-term deterioration of tenant quality, but we aren't seeing this play out yet.

2026 could be a pivotal year for REITs, and O, as interest rates are expected to further decline and the positive spread between debt/return could widen.

The dividend yield remains very attractive, and so is its valuation. We could expect 9-10% annual returns onwards, prompting me to see the stock still as a go-to for income-seeking investors, despite years of lackluster performance.

data.title: Madison Covered Call & Equity Income Fund Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Tickers

Class R6:

MENRX

Class I:

MENIX

Class Y:

MENYX

Class A:

MENAX

Fund Philosophy

Covered Call strategies, by their nature, are defensive. They are structured to knowingly sacrifice a portion of upside growth potential in order to provide additional downside protection. The Madison Covered Call & Equity Income Fund does exactly that by owning a high-quality portfolio of individual equities and selling equity call options on the portfolio holdings. The Fund offers a solid total return platform, which includes capital appreciation and a high distribution rate, which is primarily sourced from call option premiums and realized capital gains on the underlying portfolio. It is a relatively concentrated, actively managed portfolio that provides a risk-reduced way to participate in the US equity markets.

Portfolio Management

Ray Di Bernardo, CFA

Portfolio Manager, Analyst Industry since 1986

Drew Justman, CFA

Portfolio Manager, Analyst Industry since 2000

At the end of the first quarter the score is …

It’s been an eventful quarter century for equity markets. We’ve had long bullish moves interspersed with periodic gut-wrenching bear markets. The 21st century began with the massive Tech/Telecom unwind followed by a long bull market. Then came the Great Financial Crisis followed by a long bull market. Then Covid shut down the world but the market quickly bounced back stronger than ever. 2022 witnessed the most recent meaningful bear market and since then it’s been to the moon. Given recency bias most would believe that U.S. stocks, as represented by the S&P 500, would have delivered mammoth returns over the past 25 years but, in fact, the average annual return was 8.8%, below the 10.6% average return since 1957. Recent mega-cap outperformance, which has favored the S&P 500, had less impact over the full period as the S&P 500 Equal Weighted Index had a slightly higher average annual return than its market-cap weighted cousin over the full quarter century. Also, the more volatile NASDAQ 100 Index, which houses many of the most recent highflyers, averaged 10.9% annually over 25 years, a little better than the broad market.

So, while equity returns have been decent, outshining bond returns, they have not been as spectacular as many would assume. This serves as a reminder that equity markets are still cyclical. The last 3 years of very strong returns are not necessarily indicative of longer-term potential. One must always be aware of the “other” side of the cycle. Incidentally, the price of Gold has increased by 11.6% per year on average over the past quarter century. Not bad for a so called “safe haven” investment.

Q4 2025 Performance Review

The overall stock market has been on a tear since the tariff fears of early April, driven mainly by mega-cap growth stocks and anything related to artificial intelligence. That trend continued into the fourth quarter but showed signs of losing steam as a few blooms came off the AI rose. The S&P 500 gained 2.7% but it took a late rally to get into positive territory. When the dust settled, the index closed out the year marginally below its all-time high, posting a full year return of 17.9%. The CBOE S&P 500 Buy-Write Index (BXM) uncharacteristically beat the S&P 500 in a rising environment, managing to gain 6.5% in the quarter. The timing of short-term index hedges worked in its favor, particularly as the market dipped just prior to the November and December option expirations. For the full year, the BXM rose 8.9%. The Fund (Class Y)(MENYX) gained 1.6% during the quarter and 6.7% for the full year. Despite outperforming the BXM Index benchmark in each of the first three quarters of the year, the Fund was unable to match the index’s unusual fourth quarter performance. As the Fund’s option hedges are longer-dated than the BXM index, it was not able to capture the same timing benefits as the index.

From an income perspective, the Fund distribution over the full year amounted to an 8.2% yield based on the Fund’s average Net Asset Value over the time period. This yield was at the high end of our historic range and, as usual, did not incorporate any return of capital.

Given the overall market’s expensive valuation, coupled with exuberant expectations around AI, the Fund remained defensively postured. Although some cracks in the market rally became apparent, the bull has not yet broken. The Fund ended the period with 86% call option coverage, which has been consistently high throughout the year. Given the higher rate of volatility during the quarter, the option coverage was a positive contributor to returns, although negative over the full year. The Fund’s cash holding was mildly detractive to performance, which is normally the case in a rising market.

Performance data shown represents past performance. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate, so that fund shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Past performance does not guarantee future results and current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data shown. Visit madisonfunds.com or call 800.877.6089 to obtain performance data current to the most recent month-end.

Sector positioning was relatively neutral during the quarter. The Fund’s overweighting in the Health Care sector was positive as this area led the market. In addition, the underweighting in the Information Technology sector was also additive as tech stocks slightly lagged the overall market return. These benefits were largely offset by the Fund’s underweighting in the Communication Services sector, which was among the top performers driven mainly by a big gain in Alphabet. The Fund’s overweighting in the defensive Consumer Staples sector was also a minor detractor.

While the “Magnificent 7” mega-cap growth stocks beat the market overall, their performances were mixed. Alphabet (GOOGL) was, by far, the best performer, with Apple and Amazon also beating the market. However, Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta (META) saw their stock prices decline during the quarter. Within the Fund, individual stock performance was detractive to relative returns. Payment and banking service companies such as Fiserv (FISV) and PayPal (PYPL) were among the laggards, with Fiserv missing quarterly earnings targets and materially guiding down future growth expectations, which was much worse than feared.

T-Mobile (TMUS) also underperformed as multiples compressed somewhat reflecting uncertainty around the company’s CEO transition and increased satellite competition. Other marginal laggards included Comcast and insurance broker Marsh & McLennan (MRSH). Comcast was sold during the quarter and Marsh & McLennan announced good earnings but with some weakness in its re-insurance business.

On the positive side, Las Vegas Sands (LVS) continued to execute well in its Macau and Singapore hotel and gambling markets where it enjoys market leading positions. Deep water offshore driller Transocean (RIG) rebounded during the quarter as its future rig backlog remains an industry leader and rig pricing is holding up very well in a choppy global oil market. Utility AES (AES) also bounced back well as AI power demand has been the talk of the town, with the company being among the leaders in alternative energy generation for industrial usage. Keysight Technologies (KEYS) also performed well reporting strong Q4 earnings and a big uptick in new orders driven by data center customers. The company provides electronic measurement components to the telecom industry and other industrial customers in the defense, automotive, semi-conductor, government and energy sectors.

Outlook

The bull market has remained stubbornly intact despite valuations hovering at or near record highs. While the potential of “AI” continues to be a driving force behind recent market returns, concerns are increasingly gaining attention centered on the ability of “AI” applications to generate a reasonable rate of return given the vast amount of capital that has already been expended on creating the infrastructure with much more capital spending on the horizon. Questions have also swirled about the high energy requirements for driving “AI” applications and potential competitive threats mainly emanating from China. “AI” momentum has stalled somewhat recently but has not yet begun to normalize. We see the possibility of greater volatility in the “AI” environment as we move ahead, which could turn this market driver from a tailwind to a headwind.

In the rest of the “real” economy, we worry most about the consumer’s ability to keep spending at the pace of recent years. During the Covid shutdown, consumers not only reduced spending but significantly repaired their balance sheets. Outstanding credit card debt fell 17% during the shutdown but has since significantly surpassed previous pre-Covid highs by more than 33%. This while the interest rate burden on outstanding balances has surged from 15% in 2020 to over 21% currently. A similar trend is in place for auto loans and default rates are rising. All of this is occurring in a relatively stable employment market. If the consumer is barely keeping their chins above water in a stable market, consider the possibilities if the employment environment worsens. As a reminder, Consumer expenditures account for 69% of total Gross Domestic Product.

So, we ask ourselves, given the current environment should we abandon our defensive posture, hoping that the bull market will continue or do we remain cautious focusing on income generation and steady total return. Given our defensive investment mandate, we feel very strongly that protecting against downside risk is the appropriate stance.

Respectfully,

Ray Di Bernardo

Drew Justman

data.title: Headwater Exploration: Production Maintenance Goal For 2026
data.description:
Headwater Exploration (CDDRF) has been having a great year, as was covered in the last article. As was reported in that last article, exploration success continues as costs continued to decline. All of that good news augurs well for the long-term growth of this small company. Additionally, the very low costs mean that this company is unlikely to suffer to the extent of a lot of competition if the market is correct about a recession on the way. That would well mean this company will grow production right through any downturn that occurs. This is that rare growth story in a cyclical industry.

The latest press release noted the usual large annual increase in reserves. The key to this being relevant is the low cost of not only finding those reserves but also the low cost of developing those reserves. Reserve reports have limitations that mean that investors have to be very careful about reserve report conclusions. The success of the company's waterflood projects has lowered overall production costs to the point that this may be that rare situation where the company has decent cash flow even if commodity prices are weak and the discount to light oil expands (due to that pricing weakness). Note that this idea has not yet been “tried out.” But it is looking more and more viable as the Clearwater heavy oil play develops.

Clearwater E

Furthermore, the company has a discovery and now production in an area where the API for the oil discovered now goes past the “magic” 20 measurement. The API for the oil in this interval is in the 21 to 23 range. Now if the volume becomes significant, there is a chance that oil could be sold as medium-grade oil, which could net the company a little more money per barrel.

One of the things about this interval is that oil quality is mentioned above. Now at what point management can try to get more money for this production is anyone's guess. The overwhelming production is classed as heavy oil, and this is only a little bit better. But it does cross a threshold for industry management. As I have mentioned in the past, when the oil reaches the grade above shown, it is worth monitoring to see what management can do with this production in the future compared to the usual heavy oil production.

Production

The latest announcement shows production that is very roughly about what the production goal is for fiscal year 2026. This is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars. The issue here is the reality of Canada and the United States still bargaining over trading issues that are causing industry uncertainty and may bring about a North American recession.

The Venezuelan headlines about heavy oil are basically a non-issue for reasons I covered in this article. It may make the headlines and scare investors. But the reality is that no practical way to make this happen has yet appeared. Even if it does happen, it is likely to happen very slowly over several years, which will not disrupt much of anything.

But what is a consideration is the potential recession. Heavy oil producers often suffer more during recessions because the discount to light oil often expands. This makes heavy oil earnings more volatile (and volatile in the wrong direction during downturns).

This continues to be one of the most profitable upstream companies I follow. The key reason is shown above concerning maintenance cost. One of the reasons that the cost is declining while production is climbing is that secondary recovery is initiated relatively quickly here.

But that secondary recovery still results in much lower decline rates than are typical for the unconventional or even the conventional business. With roughly half of the production from secondary recovery, that production is generating a fair amount of free cash flow. Low-cost secondary production is very unusual (but is prevalent in Clearwater). The rate of the corporate average decline rate is a mixture of regular upstream production and secondary recovery. The result is a low corporate decline rate shown above.

Lots Of Payouts

The result of the strategy chosen is a rather generous payout outlook as shown below:

Compare this to the Dallas Federal Reserve Energy Survey, where most respondents stated that they needed a WTI oil price in the upper $60s before considering drilling. This company is dealing with a price that is discounted to that WTI price (though the weak Canadian dollar helps considerably), and it still is registering all those guided payouts at these low prices.

Very few companies that I deal with are this profitable in the current pricing environment, let alone considering that this company does not even get that WTI price in Canadian dollars. This is a situation where the company remains very profitable in the current environment and can become even more profitable if oil prices recover cyclically.

What helps here is the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion (pipeline) project that is helping to keep the discount on the low side. But even if there were to be a flood of heavy oil on the market, there is a clear profit advantage to this heavy oil when one sees all those payouts in the current environment.

Summary

Headwater Exploration is an extremely profitable company even given the currently weak oil prices that have the United States upstream business reporting shrinkage or declining activity to the Dallas Federal Reserve in the latest fourth quarter report (for the second quarter in a row). The reason for this is that the heavy oil basins that this operator has leases in are unusually profitable in which to operate.

The current payout guidance likely means that this small company will fare a lot better in any cyclical downturn than is the case for much of the industry. Additional considerations are the debt-free balance sheet and cash balance that I have written about many times. The profitability levels covered in this article may well mean that the debt-free balance sheet situation will continue for the foreseeable future.

The idea continues to be a strong buy because the company is very profitable. The management experience of building and selling companies, combined with no long-term debt and the cash balance, lowers the small company's risk considerably.

This is one small company that manages to both grow and pay a dividend. It is therefore likely to appeal to a wide variety of investors despite its relatively small size.

Risks

At some point, the boundaries of some of these lease areas that the company has been growing the business in will become apparent. That could potentially mark the end of a very significant growth of the industry in Canada.

But the price/earnings ratio is very conservative for the past growth of this company. That means that the market is pricing in the current uncertainties and likely the recession that is anticipated. But it is not pricing in the anticipated cyclical recovery that will likely occur.

A management that builds and sells companies is a relatively rare management. Therefore, the loss of key personnel could prove to be very challenging to replace. Most likely it would set back the future of the company.

Keep in mind that the future commodity prices have very low visibility. Therefore, the commodity price outlook can change materially without much notice either way. However, a severe and sustained decline would materially alter the future of the company.
