data.title: Strategy Looks Interesting With An mNAV To Bitcoin Of 1.05
data.description:
After more than a decade of being in existence, the idea of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) becoming an investable asset class has become normalized, especially with a market cap that sits at $1.82 trillion. Strategy (MSTR) looks interesting with a multiple of net asset value (mNAV) of 1.05x. The net asset value is the estimated value of the underlying asset, and the mNAV is the market value/NAV. In this case for MSTR, the mNAV is (Bitcoin holdings × BTC price) + cash/other assets − debt − preferred liquidation value (and other liabilities). When the mNAV exceeds a level of 1.0x, it means that MSTR is trading at a premium to its underlying asset. At one point, MSTR traded at an mNAV that exceeded 2, and investors are getting a much better value on the most popular alternative to owning BTC-USD on its own. MSTR is essentially a BTC-USD treasury vehicle layered on top of a legacy enterprise software business. MSTR is tied to the price of BTC-USD, as it owns 709,715 BTC-USD. While MSTR still operates an enterprise software business, the market looks at it as a proxy to BTC-USD. MSTR maintains a dashboard on its website that discloses its BTC-USD holdings and other key performance indicators of the business. If you believe that BTC-USD will rebound from here, MSTR could be an interesting alternative to owning BTC directly, as it has received much larger multiples on its mNAV compared to where it is today. After looking into MSTR, I am getting more interested as the mNAV makes more sense than it previously did.

How MSTR raises capital for its flywheel and what the implications are

BTC-USD isn’t cheap, and it takes real capital to acquire it. MSTR doesn’t generate billions in profitability to add more BTC-USD to its stockpile, so it needs to raise liquidity from multiple instruments so it can add BTC-USD to its balance sheet. The first way that MSTR has raised capital has been with at-the-market (ATM) common stock offerings. Recently, on 1/5/26, MSTR issued an 8-K with the SEC that disclosed ATM sales activity and explicitly stated that the BTC-USD purchases in the reported period were made using proceeds from the sale of shares of MSTR stock. Looking through the filings, this has been a core mechanism that allowed MSTR to build its exposure to BTC-USD. They have basically diluted shareholders by adding additional shares under the premise that future appreciation in BTC-USD would outstrip the dilution incurred by shareholders.

In addition to dilution through issuing common shares, MSTR has tapped the debt markets through convertible senior notes and preferred stock. Convertible notes have been a core component of MSTR’s strategy because they can reduce near-term cash interest expenses compared to just taking on traditional debt. They also provide MSTR with a call option on the equity. Recently, MSTR completed a $2.0 billion offering of 0% convertible senior notes due 2030 in February 2025. MSTR also issues preferred stock, which is backed by BTC-USD, which pays the buyers a dividend. MSTR has issued multiple preferred series with different terms that impact MSTR’s cash needs. I have outlined the 3 series of preferred stock that MSTR has issued below:

  • STRK accumulates 8.00% cumulative dividends on a $100 liquidation preference, payable when or if declared
  • STRC, a variable rate preferred series with dividends payable monthly when/if declared
  • STRD disclosed as a 10.00% Series A preferred offering

While MSTR is able to raise capital to acquire additional BTC-USD, it comes at the cost of interest on debt and dividends on preferred stock. In a September 30, 2025, Form 8-K, MSTR disclosed $8.24 billion of aggregate indebtedness. MSTR has $8.24 billion in debt and needs to pay $876 million in annualized dividends. Many investors and critics have asked if MSTR will need to sell BTC to pay their debt obligations. In the recent 8-K that MSTR filed at the beginning of 2026, MSTR stated that it maintains a U.S. dollar reserve to support preferred dividends and interest on indebtedness. They also disclosed that their reserve balance was $2.25 billion. This doesn’t eliminate risk, but it changes the narrative and lowers the probability that MSTR would find itself in a position where they would be a forced seller to meet their obligations if the price of BTC-USD declines.

Why MSTR is starting to look interesting as a proxy on Bitcoin to me

While owning BTC-USD is simple and provides direct ownership in the main asset class for MSTR, there are several reasons why investors prefer to hold MSTR. MSTR can issue equity or debt in the form of convertible notes and preferred stock to add additional BTC-USD to its balance sheet. When capital is raised at favorable terms, MSTR can increase its BTC-USD per share faster than they are diluting shareholders. This creates a scenario where MSTR can outperform BTC-USD on an appreciation basis. Some investors also don’t have accounts with brokerages that allow them to buy BTC-USD directly, and allocating capital toward MSTR allows them to have indirect exposure to BTC-USD as an asset class. The other aspect that becomes interesting for investors is that when BTC-USD rallies, MSTR has new opportunities to add additional BTC-USD as funding windows open. This puts MSTR in a situation where they can keep raising accretive capital and compounding the BTC-USD on their balance sheet. Individual investors don’t necessarily have the ability to utilize this method unless they are willing to utilize margin.

MSTR becomes interesting during durable BTC-USD trends as it will increase MSTR’s treasury value and improve the coverage ratio on its debt. MSTR also gains favorable conditions to issue more equity, which is dilutive to purchase additional BTC-USD. In an uptrend, the appreciating value of BTC can improve the mNAV for MSTR and cause its shares to appreciate quicker than owning BTC-USD directly. The fact that MSTR has backstopped its debt to a degree takes the notion off the table that they would need to liquidate BTC-USD to fulfill debt obligations. MSTR has proven that it can consistently translate equity demand into BTC-USD accumulation without destabilizing the capital stack. Currently at a BTC-USD price of $89,616, MSTR’s BTC-USD reserves are valued at $63.66 billion, and the debt of $8.24 billion places the mNAV at 1.05x. As BTC-USD continues to remain volatile on the downward decline, MSTR is starting to look very interesting as investors are able to gain access to MSTR close to the spot price on BTC-USD.

Risks to investing in MSTR

While I am starting to get interested in MSTR, there are several risks that investors should consider. Please do your own due diligence, as MSTR is a volatile asset that has been declining as the price of BTC-USD falls. MSTR can underperform BTC-USD if the market gets bearish on MSTR’s funding methods to acquire additional BTC-USD. MSTR’s strategy of issuing common shares and debt may only work if the issuance grows its BTC-USD economics per share, which would need a large uptrend to do. MSTR’s 8-K disclosures showed a large unrealized digital asset impact, which can influence sentiment even if BTC-USD appreciates in value. Investors should understand how MSTR operates before considering adding it to their portfolio.

Conclusion

It feels like crypto winter is upon us, as BTC-USD has fallen -12.18% over the past year and by -28.89% from its 52-week high of $126,223.32. This has caused a crash in MSTR, which has declined by -58.87% over the past year. I don't believe that BTC-USD is a fad that is going away, and after looking into the metrics of MSTR, I am getting interested in this investment. The fact that they are backstopping the debt to a degree with over $2 billion provides a lot of breathing room on the interest obligations. As the mNAV gets closer to 1x, I am starting to see value to be unlocked in shares of MSTR. This is now on my watchlist, and if it continues to fall to where I can start a position at a discount to mNAV, I would start to get bullish as I believe that BTC-USD will rebound by a large amount in the future.

data.title: Will The Supreme Court Expand Trump's Influence Over The Fed?
data.description:
It comes down to the meaning of two words: “for cause.” Hanging in the balance: the President’s ability to control how the Federal Reserve conducts monetary policy.

The Federal Reserve Act states that the President can fire a Fed board member “for cause.” Exactly what that means has been vague, in no small part because no President has ever removed a Fed governor. Clarifying what that means, or doesn’t, is at the core of the case before the Supreme Court. Hanging in the balance: more than a century of law and precedent, along with the degree of freedom from government (political) influence over how it sets monetary policy.

The backstory: Trump has been pushing the Fed to lower interest rates since he returned to office a year ago. As one example, in a social media post earlier this month, he wrote: “Jerome ‘Too Late’ Powell should cut interest rates, meaningfully!!!”

At issue in the case that is scheduled to go before the High Court today is the President’s attempt to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, arguably as part of an effort to remake the Fed and install new governors that will be amenable to lowering interest rates to a degree that satisfies Trump. Whatever the motivation, in August Trump wrote on his social media account: “you are hereby removed” from the role of Fed governor, in a reference to Cook. The announcement marked the first time since the Fed was created in 1913 that a Fed governor had been fired, or in this case an attempt to remove Cook.

US District Judge Jia Cobb in September ruled that Cook, who refused to resign, could remain at the Fed while she defended herself against allegations that she committed mortgage fraud – charges made by Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte, a Trump appointee.

The Supreme Court will hear arguments related to Trump’s petition to reverse a lower court judge’s ruling that prevents ​him from firing Cook while her case moves through the legal system, so the removal continues.

A related development, although it’s not formally part of the Cook case, is the Trump administration’s launch this month of a criminal investigation into Fed Chair Powell regarding testimony in Congress last year about a Fed building project. Powell responded by issuing a video statement, framing the Department of Justice investigation as a decision as an attack on the central bank’s ability “to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions — or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.”

“The Court has to tell us what a fireable offense looks like for members of the Federal Reserve Board,” says Lev Menand, an economics law scholar at Columbia Law School and former Treasury Department official. “But it can’t be just whatever the president says it is.”

Ultimately, “This is a case that’s about much more than Cook,” Menand continues. “It’s about whether President Trump will be able to take over the Federal Reserve Board in the coming months, and the administration has been pretty clear about that.”

Betting markets are currently pricing in low odds that Cook will be removed as Governor. Kalshi’s estimate this morning of ousting is 29% by the end of this month, falling to 6% through March. Polymarket is reporting similarly low odds of removal.

Meanwhile, the business of the Fed goes on, including monetary policy. The Fed funds futures market is currently estimating a high probability that the central bank will leave its target rate unchanged at next week’s policy meeting (Jan. 28).

If correct, the news may further anger President Trump. The larger question is how or if the Supreme Court decision reshuffles monetary policy expectations and decisions, and, in turn, how or if that spills over into the pricing of Treasury yields?

Whatever the outcome of the court case, it appears that politics, law and monetary policy are melding. At risk is the Fed’s autonomy, and by extension, the integrity of the financial system, real or perceived. In short, the case before the Supreme Court is much more than a decision about whether Lisa Cook can keep her job.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cencora: High-Growth Specialty Services Firm Disguised As A Distributor
data.description:
Pharmaceutical distributors have traditionally been seen as a low-margin utility providing a service to support the volume-based needs of the healthcare industry. While Cencora (COR) has historically operated in this manner, it seems the company is successfully undertaking a transformation of its business model that has not yet been recognized by the marketplace. The company is transitioning the focus of its business from the traditional wholesale logistics model to a higher margin specialty services model, a biopharmaceutical commercialization model, and an oncology support model. It is not simply rebranding itself but is repositioning its business to utilize different types of capital than in the past. I believe the best evidence of this is through the planned accelerated acquisition of OneOncology and the sale of non-core assets.

Recent Financial Updates

During the fourth quarter, the company reported $83.7 billion in total revenue, which represented a 5.9% year-over-year increase in total revenue. While high single-digit increases in revenue may be viewed as acceptable, the more compelling storyline appears to be in the financial statements beneath the income statement. The consolidated adjusted operating income for the fourth quarter increased 20.2% to $1.0 billion. The difference in the percentage change in revenue versus the percentage change in operating income reflects a successful mix shift toward higher-value services.

The full fiscal year 2025 results further support the trend of a successful conversion of top-line volume to bottom-line profits. Total revenue for the fiscal year increased 9.3% to $321.3 billion compared to the previous fiscal year's total revenue, while the adjusted diluted earnings per share for the fiscal year increased 16.3% to $16.00. These results reflect that Cencora has been successful in converting top-line volume to bottom-line profits at an increasing rate. Cencora generated $3.0 billion in adjusted free cash flow for the fiscal year and utilized some of those funds to make strategic capital expenditures.

When examining the segment performance, we are able to determine the primary sources of the strength of the company. The U.S. Healthcare Solutions segment was the most substantial contributor to the success of the company, as the segment reported a 5.7% increase in revenue to $75.8 billion for the fourth quarter. Furthermore, the operating income for the segment increased 25.1% to $0.9 billion. According to management, the growth in operating income for the segment is due to a combination of overall market growth from unit volumes of specialty products sold to health systems and physicians and the sales of GLP-1 products to treat diabetes and obesity. Gross profit expansion from the acquisition of Retina Consultants of America in January 2025 was another contributing factor to the growth in operating income; however, these were offset by an increase in operating expenses. A 25.1% increase in operating income for the segment indicates that the company's entry into the specialty and practice management segments is providing results.

Conversely, the International Healthcare Solutions segment experienced difficulties. Revenue for the segment increased 7.6% as reported to $7.9 billion for the fourth quarter; however, the operating income for the segment declined 2.0% to $150.7 million. The decline in operating income for the segment, when adjusted for constant currency, was larger at 6.0%. The decline in operating income for the segment was primarily attributable to the decline in operating income from the specialized consulting service business; however, there were positive growth trends in other business units in the segment. Although the weakness in international markets is a concern that I will continue to monitor, the size and profitability of the U.S. segment greatly outweigh the adverse effects of the international weakness.

That said, the fiscal 2026 guidance continues to be strong. The company projects revenue growth of 5% to 7% and adjusted operating income growth of 8% to 10%. The adjusted diluted earnings per share guidance of $17.45 to $17.75 represents solid growth before factoring in the long-term accretive benefits of the OneOncology acquisition.

The OneOncology Acquisition

The last big push from Cencora has been the acquisition deal they have been working on. Cencora signed a definitive agreement to purchase the majority of the remaining equity interests in OneOncology that it does not currently own; from the price Cencora paid for these shares, the enterprise was valued at $7.4 billion. Cencora's decision to buy a majority stake in OneOncology is the biggest bet yet by the company on community oncology – an area that needs high touch specialty distribution and practice management services.

I see this as a transformational event for three distinct reasons. First, the transaction directly enhances Cencora's status as a leading player in the specialty pharmaceutical space by embedding OneOncology's management services organization within the clinical and operational workflow of independent oncology practices. A relationship with an MSO that is embedded within the day-to-day operations of a physician group is much stickier than a traditional vendor-client distribution contract. Second, the transaction demonstrates alignment with Cencora's previously stated objective to lead with market leaders. OneOncology is one of the best platforms in the industry, and by purchasing a controlling stake, Cencora will be able to monetize the entire economic benefit of the platform's future growth.

Most importantly, the purchase triggered Cencora to raise its long term guidance. Cencora raised its long term adjusted operating income growth rate guidance from a range of 6% to 9% to a range of 7% to 10%, and it raised its long term adjusted diluted earnings per share growth rate guidance from a range of 9% to 13% to a range of 10% to 14%. Historically, distributors have been guiding their long term earnings growth to the high single digits. When a distributor sees a double digit floor on its long term earnings per share growth rate guidance, it is a strong indication to the street that the distributor's margins are permanently trending higher. Although the company indicated that the acquisition is expected to be approximately neutral to adjusted earnings per share in the first 12 months after closing due to the financing associated with the transaction, it is the long term value creation opportunity that is driving the current multiple.

Specialty Services and Therapy Opportunities

The recent Pharmacy Outlook report provided by Cencora gives valuable background information for why the company is investing so heavily into their specialty service offering. The report identifies a major inefficiency in today's healthcare system: 50 percent of specialty prescriptions are being handled outside of the health system today because they cannot be processed internally due to payer network limitations or patients' inability to afford them. This represents an enormous amount of lost revenue for health systems. Cencora's Accelerate Pharmacy Solutions initiative will enable health systems to recapture this volume of prescriptions by providing the necessary infrastructure to handle the processing of these prescriptions within the health system. In doing so, Cencora will further solidify its relationship with its hospital-based customers and gain control over the distribution economics of these high-cost medications.

Additionally, the report states that the global market for cell and gene therapies is projected to grow to $117.46 billion by 2034; however, only four percent of the health system respondents surveyed believe that they have the capability to properly integrate these types of therapies into their practice. The gap identified here represents a competitive moat for Cencora. Cell and gene therapies require extremely sophisticated logistics to administer (cryogenic storage, strict chain of custody, etc.) as well as complex reimbursement requirements. Cencora's announced commitment to invest $1 billion in its U.S. distribution network, which includes cold chain capabilities, is intended to serve this need.

Another growth opportunity is the home infusion market, which is expected to double in size by 2034. Two-thirds of the health systems surveyed do not have a home infusion program. Cencora's ability to provide the back-end logistics required for home infusion will allow it to capitalize on the growing trend of healthcare delivery moving out of the acute setting and into the community setting. I consider these investments as growth capital rather than maintenance capital expenditures, and they create a barrier to entry for competitors without the scale to manage the complexities associated with managing large-scale supply chains.

Valuation and Sector Context

The largest discrepancy I see in Cencora's valuation is in the revenue multiples. Cencora is trading at a forward price-to-sales ratio of 0.20 as opposed to the median of the healthcare sector of 4.25. Additionally, Cencora's enterprise value-to-sales ratio is 0.22 as opposed to the median of the healthcare sector of 3.77. Due to lower gross margin rates, distributors typically have lower sales multiples; however, the disparity between Cencora and the median health care distributor indicates the high volume of cash flow passing through the company. As Cencora increases the percentage of their business related to higher-margin services such as OneOncology and specialty consulting, each additional dollar of revenue will be worth much more. I believe the marketplace is currently treating Cencora's sales as if they were lower-quality dollars generated by a distributor and failing to recognize the increasingly higher-quality service revenue component.

Counterarguments and Risks

In the short term, the largest risk will be the leverage related to the OneOncology deal. Cencora is funding approximately $5.0 billion in cash consideration through new debt financing and is retiring $1.3 billion in outstanding OneOncology debt. This dramatically affects the overall capital structure of the company. Management has clearly stated that it is pausing all share repurchases until it can focus on deleveraging. Investors that were relying on share repurchases to create a stable floor of EPS growth are exposed to potential volatility should the operational performance of the company deteriorate.

Another key risk factor is integration risk. The merger of a large management services organization such as OneOncology, along with the merger of Retina Consultants of America, is an extremely difficult operational task. Both of these organizations are physician-driven; therefore, cultural conflicts or disruptions to the physicians' prescribing patterns may result in the loss of the exact same physicians that Cencora is paying a premium to purchase. If the synergies that are expected to develop from clinical trial development and the use of data by both organizations do not develop, the premium that was paid to purchase these companies will likely have a negative effect on returns.

Of course, regulatory risk is always present in the healthcare industry. According to Cencora’s recent report, 81% of pharmacy executives said that Medicare and Medicaid changes had a significant impact on their business. Any legislation passed that aggressively reduces reimbursement rates for specialty oncology products or changes the buy-and-bill model would negatively affect the profits of the practices that Cencora now owns and operates. In addition, the weakness of the International Healthcare Solutions segment, particularly the decline in operating income, indicates that global macroeconomic conditions or specific reimbursement pressures in Europe could continue to negatively affect the consolidated results of the company, potentially offsetting the positive effects of the domestic businesses.

Conclusion and Future Outlook

Despite these risks, I believe the transition that Cencora is experiencing is extremely beneficial for the company. Cencora is making aggressive moves in oncology and specialty practice management, which will allow them to escape the limitations of a traditional wholesale distributor model. With the acquisition of the last portion of OneOncology and the current strategic review of non-core assets, Cencora's management has demonstrated their willingness to make large capital allocation decisions to ensure a growth-oriented future.

This strategy was quantitatively validated when the company revised its long-term earning estimates to the 10-14% range. Although there are some things to monitor (the pause in buyback activity and the increase in debt), I have confidence in the fundamentals of the business and the sizeable cash flow generation capability of the company to provide a buffer against these issues and will be following future developments closely.
